worth_it_explanation: Responsive search ads are the only standard search ad format Google Ads still builds, so the assets in them are the entire creative surface a search campaign has. A thin asset set or heavy pinning narrows the combinations Google can test, and the same asset signals feed Smart Bidding, so a weak ad reads twice, once as lower click-through and once as a smaller pool for the bidding to optimize across. The checks are quick. Ad Strength, the pinning view, and the count of ads per ad group all read from the Ads tab, and the fixes are asset edits rather than a rebuild.
intro: A responsive search ad (RSA) lets the advertiser supply up to 15 headlines and 4 descriptions, and Google Ads assembles and tests combinations to find the ones that perform. The format rewards a broad, distinct asset set and a light touch on pinning because both decide how many combinations Google can try. These checks look at whether the ads carry enough distinct assets, whether pinning has been used in a way that defeats the testing, and whether each ad group has more than one ad to learn from.

audit_checks.title: Responsive search ads show 'Poor' or 'Incomplete' Ad Strength from too few or repetitive assets
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A thin or repetitive asset set leaves Google Ads few combinations to test, which holds back click-through and feeds Smart Bidding a smaller pool of signals to optimize across. Google reports that advertisers who improve Ad Strength from 'Poor' to 'Excellent' see 12% more conversions on average, so the gap is a measurable share of performance rather than a cosmetic rating.

educate.base:
Ad Strength is the feedback rating Google Ads shows on each responsive search ad, running from 'Incomplete' through 'Poor', 'Average', 'Good', and 'Excellent'. It reads the number of headlines and descriptions supplied, how distinct they are from one another, and whether the headlines include the ad group's keywords. A rating of 'Poor' or 'Incomplete' points to an asset set that is too small or too repetitive for Google to test in a useful way.

The rating is not a direct ranking factor, so a 'Poor' ad still serves. The cost is indirect. Each headline and description is an asset Google mixes into combinations, and a thin set leaves few combinations to try. Google reports that advertisers who raise Ad Strength from 'Poor' to 'Excellent' see 12% more conversions on average, drawn from a wider pool of combinations to optimize. That same pool feeds Smart Bidding, so a weak ad narrows both the creative testing and the signals the bidding learns from.

A few signs point to an asset set that earns a low rating:
  • Fewer than 8 to 10 headlines, well short of the 15 the format accepts
  • Headlines that repeat the same phrase with small wording changes rather than distinct angles
  • One or two descriptions where the format accepts four
  • Headlines that never include the ad group's main keyword
You may want to read the 'Improve Ad Strength' suggestions Google attaches to each ad since they name the specific gap, for example 'Add more headlines' or 'Make your headlines more unique', rather than only the overall grade.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, distinct headlines can lean on product range, price, shipping, and returns, each a different reason to click rather than four restatements of the brand name. You may want to confirm the ad group's headlines cover more than one of these angles since a set that repeats the category name in slightly different words reads as repetitive to Ad Strength and gives Google little to test.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, distinct headlines can speak to the offer, the proof, the speed of response, and the call to action, rather than four variations of the service name. You may want to confirm the headlines describe more than one selling point because a set built around a single phrase limits both the rating and the combinations Google can serve to different searchers.

learn_more.source_title: About Ad Strength for responsive search ads

investigate.intro: Read the Ad Strength rating on each responsive search ad and check the asset count and distinctness behind any 'Poor' or 'Incomplete' grade.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Campaigns', then 'Ads', and add the 'Ad strength' column if it is not already shown.
Sort by Ad Strength and note every responsive search ad rated 'Poor' or 'Incomplete', recording the campaign and ad group.

Open an affected ad and count its headlines and descriptions. Read the 'Improve Ad Strength' suggestions and note which gap Google names, for example too few headlines or headlines that are too similar.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per responsive search ad, the Ad Strength rating, the headline count, the description count, and the specific improvement Google suggests. An ad rated 'Good' or 'Excellent' with a full or near-full asset set passes. An ad rated 'Poor' or 'Incomplete', or one with fewer than 8 headlines or repetitive headlines, is a fail recorded with the ad group name and the gap Google flagged.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Ad Strength rating and the headline and description counts for each responsive search ad above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that several responsive search ads on {{site_name}} carry a 'Poor' or 'Incomplete' Ad Strength rating, driven by too few headlines or headlines that repeat the same idea. The format assembles combinations from the assets supplied, so a thin set leaves Google little to test, and the same assets feed Smart Bidding. {{voice_recommend}} expanding each affected ad toward the full 15 headlines and 4 descriptions with distinct angles, and adding the ad group's main keyword to at least a few headlines. After the edits, recheck the Ad Strength column and confirm the rating moves to 'Good' or 'Excellent'.

audit_checks.title: Responsive search ads pin most assets to fixed positions, defeating the combination testing
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Pinning every headline and description to a fixed position collapses a 15-headline ad down to a handful of allowed combinations, so Google can no longer test which messages perform. The ad keeps serving, but the format's main advantage is gone, and Ad Strength drops because the available combinations shrink.

educate.base:
Pinning fixes a headline or description to a set position so it always shows there, or not at all. Position 1, 2, or 3 for headlines, and position 1 or 2 for descriptions. Google offers pinning for assets that have to appear, such as a legal disclaimer or a required brand mention, and it is a useful escape hatch for those cases.

The trade-off is that pinning narrows the combinations Google can assemble. An unpinned ad with 15 headlines and 4 descriptions yields a large set of arrangements to test. Pin one headline to position 1 and only the headlines pinned there, or left unpinned, can fill that slot. Pin assets to every position and the ad collapses to a near-fixed layout, which removes the testing the format is built around. Ad Strength reflects this since a heavily pinned ad reports fewer combinations and the rating falls.

One workable pattern keeps pinning light. When an asset truly has to appear in a spot, Google's guidance is to pin two or three assets to the same position rather than one, so that slot still has options to rotate through. A few signs suggest pinning has gone too far:
  • Every position has at least one pinned asset, leaving no slot free to test
  • A single asset is pinned alone to a position, fixing that slot to one message
  • Ad Strength dropped after pinning was applied, with a suggestion to 'unpin' assets
You may want to separate pins that exist for a real requirement from pins that were used to control wording since the second kind is usually what narrows the testing without a compliance reason behind it.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, pinning is sometimes used to lock a price or a promotional claim into a fixed spot so it always shows. You may want to confirm each pin reflects a real requirement rather than a preference for word order, and where a claim has to appear, consider pinning two or three variants to that position so the slot still has something to test.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, pinning is sometimes used to keep a regulated phrase or a required disclaimer in a set position. You may want to confirm that only the assets with a genuine compliance reason are pinned, and pin two or three of them to the same slot where the rules allow, so the ad keeps some room to rotate.

investigate.intro: Open each responsive search ad and review how many assets are pinned and to which positions, separating compliance pins from pins used to control wording.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Campaigns', then 'Ads', and edit a responsive search ad to open its asset editor.
Read the pin icon next to each headline and description and note which positions are pinned and how many assets are pinned to each.
Note any position fixed to a single asset, and check whether Ad Strength for the ad carries an 'unpin' suggestion, which signals the pinning is narrowing the testing.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per responsive search ad, how many positions are pinned, whether any position is fixed to a single asset, and whether each pin has a stated compliance reason. An ad with no pinning, or with light pinning that leaves slots free and assigns two or more assets to any pinned position, passes. An ad that pins every position, fixes a slot to one asset, or pins for wording control rather than a requirement is a fail recorded with the ad group name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the pinned positions and the reason for each pin for each responsive search ad above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that responsive search ads on {{site_name}} pin most or all of their assets to fixed positions, which collapses the combinations Google can test and lowers Ad Strength. Pinning suits assets that must appear, such as a legal line, but applied broadly it removes the testing the format depends on. {{voice_recommend}} unpinning the assets that have no compliance reason behind them, and where a slot truly must hold a required message, pinning two or three variants to that position so it still has options to rotate. After the change, confirm the ad reports more available combinations and that Ad Strength recovers.

audit_checks.title: Ad groups run a single responsive search ad with no second ad to test against
audit_checks.impact_rationale: With one ad in the group, the ad rotation setting has nothing to rotate and the account has no second variant to compare. Google's own guidance is to run at least two responsive search ads per ad group with 'Good' or 'Excellent' Ad Strength, so a lone ad falls short of the baseline the platform recommends.

educate.base:
An ad group can hold more than one responsive search ad, and Google's guidance is to run at least two with 'Good' or 'Excellent' Ad Strength. A group with a single ad gives the account nothing to compare against and gives ad rotation nothing to rotate since rotation governs how multiple ads in a group enter the auction relative to one another.

The ad rotation setting offers 'Optimize', which favors the ads expected to perform better, and 'Do not optimize', which enters ads into the auction more evenly. Neither does anything in a group with one ad. The first variant is also the only variant, so there is no read on whether a different angle would do better, and a single creative problem affects all of the group's traffic with no fallback running beside it.

A second responsive search ad does not have to be a full rewrite. A distinct second ad that tests a different lead message, a different offer, or a different call to action gives Google a real comparison. A few signs suggest a group is running too lean:
  • The ad group shows one enabled responsive search ad and no second one
  • The only other ads in the group are paused, so just one is live
  • The single ad has run unchanged for a long stretch with no variant beside it
You may want to confirm whether the lone ad is by design or a leftover from a build where a second ad was planned and never added since the fix differs between the two.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a second responsive search ad can test a different lead angle for the same ad group, for example price against shipping or selection against a current promotion. You may want to confirm each ad group running products has a second live ad since a lone ad leaves the group's traffic riding on a single creative with no comparison.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, a second responsive search ad can test a different value message for the same ad group, for example speed of response against depth of expertise. You may want to confirm the ad groups that drive form fills carry a second live ad, so the account has a real comparison rather than a single creative carrying all the traffic.

learn_more.source_title: About Ad Strength for responsive search ads

investigate.intro: Count the live responsive search ads in each ad group and note any group running a single enabled ad.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Campaigns', then 'Ads', and group or filter the view by ad group.
For each ad group, count the enabled responsive search ads, ignoring paused or removed ones.
Record every ad group with a single enabled responsive search ad, and note whether other ads exist but sit paused.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad group, the count of enabled responsive search ads. An ad group with two or more live ads passes. An ad group running a single enabled responsive search ad is a fail recorded with the ad group name, with a note on whether a second ad exists in a paused state or is absent entirely.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the count of enabled responsive search ads for each ad group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that several ad groups on {{site_name}} run a single responsive search ad, which leaves the account with no second variant to compare and leaves ad rotation with nothing to rotate. Google's guidance is at least two ads per ad group, so a lone ad sits below the recommended baseline and puts all of the group's traffic on one creative. {{voice_recommend}} adding a second distinct responsive search ad to each affected ad group, testing a different lead message or call to action, and confirming both ads reach 'Good' or 'Excellent' Ad Strength. After the addition, let both ads accrue data before judging which performs better.
